title: Tess of the d'Urbervilles
author: Thomas Hardy
overture: Tess of the d'Urbervilles walks. From the Vale of Blackmoor to the dairies of the Froom, up onto the starve-acre uplands and at last to Stonehenge, almost every mile is on foot, across a Dorset that Hardy renamed but barely disguised. Three lives cross and recross one county; the map plays them together, chapter by chapter.

sources.name: Thomas Hardy's map of 'The Wessex of the Novels' (1895)
F. B. Pinion, A Hardy Companion (1968)

regions.name: The valley of the Great Dairies

characters.name: Tess Durbeyfield
characters.role: Cottager's daughter and last of the real d'Urbervilles; nearly everything on this map she crosses on foot

characters.name: Angel Clare
characters.role: The vicar's freethinking son, learning farming at Talbothays; a year in Brazil, offstage, divides his two halves of this map

characters.name: Alec d'Urberville
characters.role: The sham d'Urberville of The Slopes - rake, then ranter, then rake again; he crosses Tess's path at long intervals, and Sandbourne is his last stop

locations.name: Marnhull, Dorset

image.caption: The Vale of Blackmoor, Dorset - the green vale Hardy renamed Blackmoor and set Marlott in - in a watercolour of about 1913
image.credit: Edward Francis Wells (1876–1952) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.note: Marnhull, by universal identification since Hardy's own 1895 Wessex map and Hermann Lea (1913).
locations.story: Tess's birthplace in the Vale of Blackmoor: the May club-walking where a young Angel Clare, passing on a walking tour, dances - though not with her. She comes home to it in trouble twice, and her father's grand unpaid-for headstone ends up in its churchyard.
locations.quote: The village of Marlott lay amid the north-eastern undulations of the beautiful Vale of Blakemore, or Blackmoor

locations.name: Shaftesbury
locations.note: Shaftesbury, by universal identification (Hardy's Wessex map; Lea 1913).
locations.story: The hill-town on the Vale's rim, shining in Tess's childhood evenings like a row of lamps. It is her gateway out of Blackmoor: the Chaseborough carrier's van starts from here.
locations.quote: above all, the town of Shaston standing majestically on its height; its windows shining like lamps in the evening sun
locations.imageBlank: No period photograph or engraved view of Shaftesbury (Hardy's Shaston) or Gold Hill exists in the public domain - only an antique town-plan, which would sit oddly beside the book's scenic images; a blank is the honest choice.

locations.name: Pentridge, Dorset
locations.note: Trantridge is universally identified with Pentridge (Hardy's map; Lea 1913). The Slopes itself - the Stoke-d'Urbervilles' bright new villa - is Hardy's invention, so the house is folded into the village here.
locations.story: The Slopes, the spick-and-span house of the Stoke-d'Urbervilles: northern money wearing a bought name. Tess comes to claim kin with people who are no kin at all, keeps Mrs d'Urberville's poultry, and is taught to whistle to bullfinches under Alec's supervision.
locations.quote: She alighted from the van at Trantridge Cross, and ascended on foot a hill in the direction of the district known as The Chase
locations.imageBlank: Trantridge is the hamlet of Pentridge and The Slopes is Hardy's invention; Commons holds no public-domain image of Pentridge, only modern photographs, so there is no honest specific picture.

locations.name: Cranborne
locations.note: Cranborne, identified since Lea (1913) - Hardy drew the novel's inn from the real Fleur-de-Lys at Cranborne.
locations.story: The decayed market-town where the Trantridge work-folk dance and drink away their Saturday nights. On a September fair-night Tess waits too long for company on the walk home, and accepts a lift from the wrong man.
locations.quote: going every Saturday night, when work was done, to Chaseborough, a decayed market-town two or three miles distant
locations.imageBlank: Chaseborough is Cranborne; there is no public-domain contemporaneous view of the town - every image of it is a modern photograph - so a logged blank is more honest than an off-period substitute.

locations.name: Cranborne Chase
locations.note: Cranborne Chase, named all but openly; the identification is universal (Hardy's map; Lea 1913).
locations.story: The oldest wood in England - primaeval yews and oaks, Druidical mistletoe. Riding home in fog, Alec loses the way here not quite honestly; what happens under the trees Hardy keeps in darkness, and it is the night that breaks Tess's life in two.
locations.quote: A bit of The Chase-the oldest wood in England.
locations.imageBlank: Cranborne Chase (Hardy's The Chase) survives in the public domain only as old boundary maps, not woodland views; rather than a map beside the scenic images, a considered blank.

locations.name: The Frome valley, near West Stafford

image.caption: The water-meadows of the Frome - Hardy's Valley of the Great Dairies, where Talbothays is set - with Dorchester beyond, in a watercolour of about 1903
image.credit: "Picturesque Dorset" postcard series, c.1903 · Newberry Library via CARLI · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.note: The dairy is invented: Lea (1913) treats it as a composite of Froom-valley dairies, the name borrowed from a Hardy family farm at West Stafford. The valley itself is certain; this point stands in the water-meadows south-east of Dorchester.
locations.story: Dairyman Crick's great dairy in the Valley of the Great Dairies, where Tess rallies. The heart of the book's one happy stretch: the dawn milkings, the garden at dusk, and Angel Clare - by harvest she has agreed, against her own conscience, to marry him.
locations.quote: she found herself on a summit commanding the long-sought-for vale, the Valley of the Great Dairies

locations.name: Woolbridge Manor, Wool

image.caption: The ruins of Bindon Abbey at Wool - the abbey by Wellbridge where the sleepwalking Angel lays Tess in a stone coffin - in a pen-and-wash drawing of 1791
image.credit: James Moore, 1791 · Yale Center for British Art · Wikimedia Commons · CC0

locations.note: Woolbridge Manor at Wool, by universal identification (Hardy's map; Lea 1913) - and a gift of a fact: the manor really belonged to the Turbervilles, the family whose decayed name Hardy borrowed.
locations.story: The honeymoon house, once a d'Urberville manor: the wedding-night confessions, the dreadful ancestral portraits on the stair, and Angel's sleepwalk with Tess in his arms to a stone coffin in the abbey ruins. The marriage lasts three days here.
locations.quote: once portion of a fine manorial residence, and the property and seat of a d'Urberville, but since its partial demolition a farmhouse

locations.name: Bere Regis

image.caption: Bere Regis - Hardy's Kingsbere - in a watercolour of 1910
image.credit: E. W. Haslehust, from The Heart of Wessex, 1910 · public domain

locations.note: Bere Regis - the real Turberville vaults and window are in the parish church, exactly as the novel describes (Hardy's map; Lea 1913).
locations.story: The ancestral parish, with rows of d'Urbervilles in the church vaults. Evicted from Marlott on Old Lady-Day, the family finds its promised rooms already let and sets up the four-post bedstead against the church wall, beneath the d'Urberville Window.
locations.quote: Hard by, the aisle of the church called the d'Urberville Aisle looked on imperturbably.

locations.name: Dorchester

image.caption: Dorchester - Hardy's Casterbridge - seen across the water-meadows, photographed about 1890–1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.note: Dorchester, the capital of Hardy's Wessex (his map; Lea 1913). Its part in this novel is a passing one.
locations.story: The county town at the edge of Tess's map: it is on the dark road to its Saturday market, with the beehives, that the mail-cart spears Prince the horse - the small disaster that sets the whole story moving.
locations.quote: if they were to be delivered to the retailers in Casterbridge before the Saturday market began

locations.name: Beaminster

image.caption: A church and its churchyard at Beaminster - Hardy's Emminster, home of the Clare vicarage - photographed in 1898
image.credit: Valentine & Sons, 1898 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.note: Beaminster, by universal identification (Hardy's map; Lea 1913).
locations.story: The Clare vicarage, Angel's home. Tess walks fifteen miles here in midwinter to ask his parents for help, and turns back at the gate - her nerve finished off by his brothers' talk on the road, and by the confiscation of her hidden walking-boots.
locations.quote: about noon she paused by a gate on the edge of the basin in which Emminster and its Vicarage lay

locations.name: Evershot
locations.note: Evershot, by universal identification (Lea 1913); the novel's Sow-and-Acorn is the real Acorn Inn.
locations.story: The halfway village on Tess's great midwinter walk: breakfast at a cottage by the church going out, a sadder halt coming back - and, a little beyond, the barn where the voice of the preaching ranter is one she knows.
locations.quote: She made a halt here, and breakfasted a second time, heartily enough-not at the Sow-and-Acorn, for she avoided inns, but at a cottage by the church
locations.imageBlank: Evershead is Evershot; Commons holds no public-domain image of the village - the real Acorn Inn and every street view survive only in modern photographs.

locations.name: Near Plush, Dorset
locations.note: The farm is invented and the site disputed: Lea (1913) placed it near Nettlecombe Tout, Kay-Robinson (1972) argued for the Doles Ash area above Plush. This point stands on that stretch of chalk upland.
locations.story: The starve-acre farm: swede-hacking in the rain, reed-drawing in snow, the relentless threshing-machine, all under a farmer with a grudge. Alec d'Urberville - newly converted, then swiftly unconverted - lays siege to her here through the winter.
locations.quote: There was no exaggeration in Marian's definition of Flintcomb-Ash farm as a starve-acre place.
locations.imageBlank: Flintcomb-Ash is an invented farm on a disputed upland site; there is no real spot to picture and no distinct public-domain chalk-upland view that isn't generic - a blank is honest for a conjectured place.

locations.name: Bournemouth

image.caption: Bournemouth - Hardy's Sandbourne - the pier and the front seen from the East Cliff, photographed about 1890–1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.note: Bournemouth, by universal identification (Hardy's map; Lea 1913). The Herons, the boarding-house where Tess is found, is invented, and is folded into the town here.
locations.story: The glittering new pleasure-city where Alec keeps Tess at The Herons, and where Angel finds her too late. Within the hour of his leaving, Alec lies dead in the upstairs room, and Tess is gone after her husband.
locations.quote: It was a city of detached mansions; a Mediterranean lounging-place on the English Channel

locations.name: The New Forest, Hampshire

image.caption: A glade in the New Forest, Hampshire - the country the fugitives cross to reach the empty house of Bramshurst Court - photographed about 1890–1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.note: Scholarship has never settled Bramshurst Court: Lea offered no identification, and later guesses (Moyles Court near Ringwood is sometimes proposed) have never stuck. This point simply stands in the New Forest, on the fugitives' northward line.
locations.story: An empty furnished mansion with the blinds down and grass growing on the drive: five days of borrowed married happiness - the only ones Tess and Angel are given - until a caretaker's morning round sends them north again.
locations.quote: It is Bramshurst Court. You can see that it is shut up, and grass is growing on the drive.

locations.name: Salisbury

image.caption: Salisbury - Hardy's Melchester - the cathedral spire rising over the city the fugitives pass through by night, photographed about 1890–1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.note: Salisbury, by universal identification (Hardy's map; Lea 1913).
locations.story: The fugitives pass through at midnight, keeping off the pavements so their footsteps will not echo, the cathedral rising dimly on their left hand - lost on them now.
locations.quote: The intercepting city, ancient Melchester, they were obliged to pass through in order to take advantage of the town bridge for crossing a large river

locations.name: Stonehenge

image.caption: Stonehenge on Salisbury Plain - where Tess sleeps on a sun-warmed slab and is taken at dawn - photographed about 1875
image.credit: Francis Frith (1822–1898) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.story: The heathen temple on the open plain, met blind in the dark. Tess sleeps on a sun-warmed slab among the pillars while Angel keeps watch; the police close in with the dawn, and she is ready - 'It is as it should be.'
locations.quote: Older than the centuries; older than the d'Urbervilles!

locations.name: Winchester

image.caption: Winchester Cathedral - Hardy's Wintoncester, the city of the trial and the hanging - photographed about 1890–1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.note: Winchester, by universal identification (Hardy's map); the prison on the city's western side still stands.
locations.story: The old capital of Wessex, where Tess is tried and hanged. Angel and 'Liza-Lu climb the great West Hill and watch, hand in hand, as the black flag moves slowly up the prison tower.
locations.quote: The city of Wintoncester, that fine old city, aforetime capital of Wessex, lay amidst its convex and concave downlands in all the brightness and warmth of a July morning

movements.note: Sent by her mother to claim kin with the rich d'Urbervilles
The twice-weekly carrier's van; she alights at Trantridge Cross
Home the same day, Alec's roses at her breast
The last miles down into the Vale on foot
To live, as keeper of Mrs d'Urberville's fowls - Alec driving the last downhill stage at a deliberately reckless gallop

via.name: Shaston (Shaftesbury)
via.note: up out of the Vale onto the hill-town

via.name: Handley (Sixpenny Handley)
via.note: along the chalk edge toward the Chase country

movements.routeNote: Up out of Blackmoor by Shaston, then east along the chalk by Handley to Trantridge - Alec driving the last downhill stage at a reckless gallop.
movements.routeSource: The carrier's road out of the Vale climbed through Shaftesbury; the line east along the downs to Pentridge follows the real lanes past Sixpenny Handley.

movements.note: Hovering about the town on the fair-night, cigar alight
The September night when fair and market coincide; her comrades dance into the small hours
She rides pillion to escape a quarrel on the dark road; in the fog he loses the way - not quite honestly
Of the return the novel says nothing
The Sunday-morning walk home with her basket, a few weeks later; Alec overtakes her and drives her part of the way

via.name: Handley (Sixpenny Handley)
via.note: west along the chalk out of the Chase country

via.name: Shaston (Shaftesbury)
via.note: the hill-town on the Vale's rim

movements.routeNote: The long walk home for good: west along the downs by Handley and over Shaston, then down into the Vale - Alec overtaking her, and put off, on the road.
movements.routeSource: The reverse of her road into service: the real lanes east of Pentridge by Sixpenny Handley, then the Shaftesbury road down into Blackmoor.

movements.note: Two years and more on: hired trap to Stourcastle, a farmer's spring-cart to Weatherbury, the last miles on foot over the heath
The wedding-day drive down the valley in the creaking old brougham, to a former d'Urberville house for the honeymoon
The parting, five days married: he sets her down within a walk of home, by Nuttlebury, and the road divides them
Three weeks after the wedding, to tell his parents of Brazil. His track pauses here: a year in Brazil, offstage, before he returns to Emminster fever-worn

via.name: Casterbridge (Dorchester)
via.note: west along the valley to the county town

via.name: Maiden Newton
via.note: up the Frome and out toward the western hills

movements.routeNote: West down the valley from Wellbridge through Casterbridge, then up by Maiden Newton to the Clare vicarage at Emminster.
movements.routeSource: No route named for the leg - the turnpike west from Wool ran through Dorchester, and Maiden Newton is the road-junction for Beaminster.

movements.note: One leg standing for a wandering summer and autumn of field-work as far west as Port-Bredy; the night among the dying pheasants falls on this road
The fifteen-mile midwinter walk to Angel's parents, past the stone at Cross-in-Hand; breakfast at a cottage by the church
By Benvill Lane, the walking-boots hidden in a hedge for the journey home
Her nerve gone at the vicarage gate - and the boots gone too, carried off for the deserving poor
Reappearing after the missing years as a ranting convert, mid-circuit, preaching in a barn on the Evershead road

via.name: Blandford Forum
via.note: west off the Cranborne country into the heart of Dorset

via.name: Buckland Newton
via.note: up the Blackmoor edge toward the western hills

movements.routeNote: Right across Dorset from the Cranborne country by Pentridge, west through Blandford Forum and up by Buckland Newton to the barn on the Evershead road.
movements.routeSource: No route named in the text - Hardy has Alec appear mid-circuit as a ranting convert. This reconstructs the natural line west across the county on the Blandford turnpike toward Evershot.

movements.note: Past the barn where the preacher is Alec d'Urberville; he follows, and at Cross-in-Hand makes her swear on the stone never to tempt him
Walking after Tess as far as Cross-in-Hand; through the late winter he besieges the farm
Summoned to her mother's sickbed: fifteen miles through Blackmoor by night
He follows - the firelit allotment plot, a pitchfork in his hand
Old Lady-Day: the family evicted, their goods on a hired waggon; the promised rooms are let, and the beds go up against the church wall
Still in pursuit: it is his form Tess finds stretched on the altar-tomb in the d'Urberville aisle
Offstage, between chapters: her family destitute, Tess yields at last and goes with Alec to keep house at The Herons - his last remove, and hers.

via.name: Wareham
via.note: down off the heath toward the coast

via.name: Poole
via.note: the harbour town at the edge of the new resort

movements.routeNote: South-east off the Kingsbere heath by Wareham and Poole to the glittering new pleasure-city of Sandbourne.
movements.routeSource: The move is passed over between chapters; the line follows the real road from Bere Regis down through Wareham and Poole to Bournemouth.

via.name: Benvill Lane
via.note: the high lane east out of the Emminster basin

via.name: Cross-in-Hand
via.note: the sinister old stone on the upland

movements.note: Home from Brazil, searching: a hired trap along Benvill Lane and past Cross-in-Hand. At the farm no 'Mrs Clare' has ever been heard of
movements.routeNote: His search runs her winter backward: a hired trap along Benvill Lane and over the hills by Cross-in-Hand to the starve-acre farm.
movements.routeSource: Hardy names Benvill Lane and Cross-in-Hand on this search (Ch. 54); the line between them follows the real upland lanes.

movements.note: A borrowed gig to the Vale's edge, the last stretch on foot - to find strangers in the house and John Durbeyfield's headstone unpaid for

via.name: the Blackmoor edge
via.note: down off the flint uplands toward the Vale

movements.routeNote: A borrowed gig north off the uplands to the Vale's edge, the last stretch on foot into Marlott.
movements.routeSource: No route named for the leg; the line drops north off the Flintcomb uplands into Blackmoor by the real lanes toward Marnhull.

movements.note: Hired beyond Shaston to Joan's new village for the address - 'She is at Sandbourne' - then the last train down
She catches him on the road out of town - 'I have done it-I don't know how' - and they walk inland until an empty furnished house takes them in
Five days' grace ended by a caretaker's morning round; they keep a bee-line north
Through the midnight streets, off the pavements, then the turnpike-road onto the open plain - and pillars rising out of the dark
Taken at dawn from the warm slab where she slept - 'I am ready'

via.name: Melchester (Salisbury)
via.note: back off the plain into the cathedral city

movements.routeNote: Taken off Salisbury Plain a prisoner: back through Melchester, then east by Romsey and the Test valley to the county gaol at Wintoncester.
movements.routeSource: The text says only that she is taken across Wessex to the county gaol. This follows the real road east from Salisbury through Romsey to Winchester.

movements.note: To the great West Hill with 'Liza-Lu, one July morning, to watch the black flag go up

via.name: Melchester (Salisbury)
via.note: off the plain, back through the cathedral city

movements.routeNote: The road east from the plain: down through Melchester and by Romsey and the Test valley to the great West Hill above Wintoncester.
movements.routeSource: No route named - Hardy brings Angel and 'Liza-Lu to the West Hill above Winchester. This follows the real Salisbury–Romsey–Winchester road.

story.title: The maiden
story.narration: Tess Durbeyfield, a cottager's daughter, dances at the May club-walking in Marlott, her village in the green Vale of Blackmoor. A young stranger, Angel Clare, passes on a walking tour and dances there - though not with her.

story.title: Prince
story.narration: Her feckless father, told he descends from the noble d'Urbervilles, drinks on the news. Tess drives the beehives to market in his place, and on the dark road the mail-cart spears the old horse, Prince. The family's living dies with him.

story.title: Sent to claim kin
story.narration: Blamed for the loss, Tess is sent by her mother to the hill-town of Shaston to seek help from rich relations who bear the d'Urberville name - people, in truth, no kin at all, only northern money that has bought a dead family's title.

story.title: The Slopes
story.narration: The carrier's van carries her on to Trantridge and the spick-and-span house called The Slopes, where she first meets Alec d'Urberville - idle, handsome, already looking at her too closely, and pressing strawberries and roses on her.

story.title: Home the same day
story.narration: She rides back to Shaston that same day, Alec's roses at her breast, uneasy about the visit but carrying an offer: they want her to come and keep the poultry.

story.title: Down into the Vale
story.narration: The last miles down into Blackmoor she walks on foot, home to a mother already set on her going. Against her own misgiving, Tess agrees to take the place.

story.title: To service
story.narration: She leaves home to keep Mrs d'Urberville's fowls. Alec drives the last downhill stage himself, at a deliberately reckless gallop, frightening her into holding on to him - the first of his small cruelties dressed as fun.

story.title: The poultry-farm
story.narration: Weeks pass at Trantridge. Tess tends the hens and is taught to whistle to the caged bullfinches under Alec's supervision. He circles her constantly; she keeps him off, but she is a servant in his mother's house, and alone.

story.title: The fair-night
story.narration: On a September night, when fair and market fall together, the Trantridge work-folk walk over to Chaseborough to drink and dance. Tess goes with them and waits too long for company on the road home.

story.title: Hovering
story.narration: Alec has been hovering about the town all evening, cigar alight, on horseback in the dark - waiting, as he always seems to be, for Tess to be tired and unguarded and far from her own door.

story.title: Into The Chase
story.narration: To escape a quarrel among the drunken walkers, Tess lets Alec take her up on his horse. In the fog he loses the way - not quite honestly - deep into The Chase, the oldest wood in England. What happens there is left in darkness.

story.title: The return
story.narration: Of the ride back the novel says nothing at all. The night in the wood has broken Tess's life in two; she stays a few more weeks at Trantridge, and then can bear it no longer.

story.title: Maiden no more
story.narration: One Sunday morning she walks home for good, basket in hand. Alec overtakes her, drives her part of the way, and offers to keep her; she refuses him and goes on alone, back into the Vale she left a girl.

story.title: The child
story.narration: At home in Marlott, shamed and silent, Tess bears Alec's child. It sickens within weeks; the parson will not come, so she baptises it herself by night, names it Sorrow, and buries it in an unhallowed corner of the churchyard.

story.title: The silent years
story.narration: Then two quiet, reconstructive years, barely told. The girl hardens into a woman; the worst, she believes, is behind her. When at last she takes work again, it is far off, in a valley she has never seen.

story.title: The valley of the dairies
story.narration: She travels south and east out of Blackmoor - a hired trap, a farmer's cart, the last miles on foot over the heath - down into the lush Valley of the Great Dairies, to milk at Talbothays. Her spirits rise with the green new country.

story.title: Angel again
story.narration: Dairyman Crick's is the book's one happy stretch: dawn milkings, the warm crowded dairy-house. And among the hands, learning farming, is Angel Clare - the vicar's freethinking son, the very man who danced past her at Marlott, though neither yet remembers the other.

story.title: The dawn milkings
story.narration: Through midsummer the two are thrown together in the grey half-light before dawn, when the meadows are theirs alone. Angel falls in love with what he takes for a fresh, unspoiled child of nature; Tess loves him back, and dreads what she has to tell.

story.title: The proposal
story.narration: By high summer Angel has asked her to marry him. Tess refuses him again and again - her past a wall she cannot climb - then, worn down by his gentleness and her own heart, against her conscience she says yes.

story.title: The letter unread
story.narration: The wedding is fixed for New Year's Eve. Tess writes out her whole history and slips it under Angel's door; it slides beneath the carpet, and he never sees it. She takes his silence for forgiveness, and lets the day come.

story.title: The wedding drive
story.narration: Married, they drive down the valley in a creaking old brougham to Wellbridge - a gaunt farmhouse that was once a d'Urberville manor - to spend the honeymoon among the grim painted faces of her own ancestors on the stair.

story.title: The confession
story.narration: That first married night, by the fire, Angel confesses a brief loose living of his own; Tess forgives it in a breath, and tells her own story in turn. The same fault - but where she pardons, he cannot. The fire goes cold between them.

story.title: Three days
story.narration: The marriage lasts three days at Wellbridge, in a courteous horror. Angel walks in his sleep, carries her in his arms across the churchyard, and lays her in a stone coffin in the ruined abbey. Awake, he has decided: they must part.

story.title: Set down near home
story.narration: He drives her back toward the Vale and sets her down within a walk of Marlott, an allowance in her hand and a vague hope of Brazil in his. The road divides; she watches him go, and does not tell her family the truth.

story.title: Angel's road
story.narration: Now follow Angel a moment. Three weeks after the wedding he goes to his father's vicarage to announce his plan - to try his fortune farming in Brazil - saying nothing of a wife he has left behind, only that he means to travel alone.

story.title: To Emminster, and away
story.narration: From Wellbridge he goes home to Emminster, and from there sails for Brazil. His track stops at the vicarage: a whole year abroad, unseen on this map, farming badly and sickening with fever, while Tess is left to shift for herself.

Meanwhile, through the same long year, Tess is passed from one hard field to another, her small money gone, too proud to beg from Angel's people. Wind back to the Vale, and follow her out onto the bleak western hills.

story.title: To the starve-acre
story.narration: One long walk stands for a wandering summer and autumn of field-work as far west as Port-Bredy. It ends on the flint uplands at Flintcomb-Ash - and on that road, by night, she finds pheasants half-dead from the guns and wrings their necks in mercy.

story.title: The hard winter
story.narration: Flintcomb-Ash is a starve-acre place: swede-hacking in the freezing rain, reed-drawing in the snow, under a farmer with an old grudge against her. With her friend Marian she endures the worst winter of her life, and still will not write to Angel.

story.title: The winter walk begins
story.narration: At last, desperate, she sets out to beg help from Angel's parents - a fifteen-mile walk in midwinter over the hills, past the sinister old stone at Cross-in-Hand, breakfasting at a cottage by the church in the village of Evershead.

story.title: To the vicarage gate
story.narration: By Benvill Lane she comes at noon within sight of Emminster and its vicarage, hiding her thick walking-boots in a hedge so as to arrive respectable - the whole hard journey resting on this one plea for kindness.

story.title: Turned back
story.narration: Her nerve fails at the gate. Overhearing Angel's brothers speak slightingly of his marriage, she flees before she can knock - and finds even her hidden boots gone, carried off as charity for the deserving poor. She walks back beaten.

story.title: The convert
story.narration: On that same road she passes a barn where a ranting preacher holds forth - and the voice is Alec d'Urberville's. The rake has turned Methodist convert, come from Trantridge on a preaching circuit straight into her path. The past she buried stands up in the road.

story.title: The oath on the stone
story.narration: She hurries on for Flintcomb-Ash and Alec follows, his new faith already shaking at the sight of her. At Cross-in-Hand he makes her lay her hand on the ugly stone and swear never to tempt him - then cannot keep away himself.

story.title: The siege
story.narration: Through the late winter Alec besieges the farm, his conversion falling from him week by week. He offers marriage, then simply pursues her over the roar of the threshing-machine. Tess, still Angel's in her own mind, holds out, and writes a despairing letter to Brazil.

story.title: Summoned home
story.narration: Word comes that her mother is dying. Tess walks fifteen miles through Blackmoor by night to reach Marlott - and Alec follows even there, finding her at work by firelight on the family allotment, a pitchfork in his own hand.

story.title: The family cast out
story.narration: Her mother rallies, but her father suddenly dies, and with him the cottage lease. On Old Lady-Day the Durbeyfields are turned out of Marlott altogether, their goods on a hired waggon - and Alec at her elbow, offering rescue on his own terms.

story.title: Under the d'Urberville window
story.narration: The family flits to Kingsbere, the ancestral parish, only to find the rooms they were promised already let. They set the four-post bedstead up against the church wall, beneath the very window of the d'Urberville vaults - the dead grand, the living destitute.

story.title: Pursuit to the vaults
story.narration: Alec has ridden after them to Kingsbere. It is his living form Tess finds stretched out on an altar-tomb in the d'Urberville aisle, mocking her ruined lineage - the last turn of the screw, with her family sleeping against the churchyard wall.

story.title: The surrender
story.narration: What happens next the book passes over between chapters. With her people destitute and no word from her husband, Tess yields at last, and goes with Alec to the bright new seaside city of Sandbourne - kept at The Herons as the price of her family's shelter.

story.title: Angel comes home
story.narration: And here, too late, Angel returns. Ruined and fever-thin, he lands from Brazil a changed man, her despairing letter having reached him at last, and comes to his father's house at Emminster resolved to find the wife he threw away.

story.title: Searching
story.narration: He sets out to trace her: a hired trap along Benvill Lane and past Cross-in-Hand to Flintcomb-Ash, where no 'Mrs Clare' has ever been heard of. The map now runs his search backward along the very roads of her winter.

story.title: To an empty house
story.narration: On to Marlott by borrowed gig and foot, only to find strangers in the Durbeyfield cottage and John Durbeyfield's fine headstone standing unpaid for. He learns the family has scattered, and that Tess is not among them.

story.title: By the last train
story.narration: Directed on to the new village of Tess's feckless mother, Joan, and given an address at last - 'She is at Sandbourne' - Angel takes the last train down to the glittering pleasure-town, and knocks at the boarding-house called The Herons in the morning.

story.title: Too late
story.narration: He finds her kept there in mourning finery, another man's. She tells him he has come too late - she waited, and gave up, and was bought. She sends him away, and he goes; and in the room above, something in her breaks for good.

story.title: The deed
story.narration: Within the hour Tess follows him out. She has killed Alec d'Urberville - stabbed him at the breakfast table, the blood staining red through the ceiling below - and gone after her husband on the road, freed by murder to be his at last.

story.title: The fugitives
story.narration: 'I have done it - I don't know how,' she tells him, and he does not turn her away. They walk inland into the New Forest and shelter in an empty furnished mansion: five borrowed days of the marriage they never had.

story.title: North by night
story.narration: A caretaker's morning round ends the idyll. They flee north on foot, keeping a bee-line across country, and at midnight reach the old cathedral city of Melchester, obliged to pass through it to cross the river by the town bridge.

story.title: Stonehenge
story.narration: Off the echoing pavements and out onto the open plain, they come in the dark upon Stonehenge - the heathen temple, older than the d'Urbervilles. Worn out, Tess lies down to sleep on a sun-warmed altar-stone while Angel keeps watch for the dawn.

story.title: 'I am ready'
story.narration: The police close in with the first light. Tess wakes, sees the ring of men, and is calm - 'It is as it should be… I am ready.' She is taken across Wessex to the county gaol at Wintoncester to stand her trial.

story.title: The last watchers
story.narration: One last thread. Angel, left behind at the stones, keeps his promise to her: that when she is gone he will care for her young sister 'Liza-Lu, so like her.

story.title: The black flag
story.narration: On a bright July morning the two climb the great West Hill above Wintoncester and look back at the prison. They wait, hand in hand; and slowly, on the tower, a black flag runs up. Tess has been hanged. They bow down, and go on.
